pages.full_text:
[photo of stone carving of angel with horn]

Santiago de Compostella
pages.notes: Page is almost entirely occupied by a glued-in photo of a Romanesque stone carving of a winged angel playing a horn/trumpet. The only handwritten text is the caption 'Santiago de Compostella' written below the photo. There appears to be very faint text above the photo that is largely obscured but may be a continuation from a previous page; it is not legible enough to transcribe. No handwritten page number visible.

pages.full_text:
lovely with their shape in the stones supporting the roof beams. St. Rocca appears often along the trail. In this chapel there were hundreds of flowers.

Later we visited Portomarin. The valley was flooded for controlling water for irrigation, + the church was brought up, stone by stone, to a higher village. The stones still had numbers on. The archit. + carving were again beautiful Romanesque.

We reached __Compostella__ + camped as __As cancelas__ ***, at 7.0.. The rain is falling, so we have not put up the tent. We set off on the bus for the city centre. The cathedral is [?wonderful]. The first place in Spain that the church seems user-friendly. [?Mass] is being celebrated, + tourists swarm all around, admiring, gawping, eating crepes, achieving their goal. Many people praying. Many walk up behind the altar to kiss the
pages.notes: Text continues from previous page (begins mid-sentence with 'lovely with their shape...'). Text is also cut off at the bottom — the sentence 'Many walk up behind the altar to kiss the' is incomplete. There is a small hand-drawn spiral/snail-like doodle on the left margin near the middle of the page. 'As cancelas' is underlined and appears to be the name of the campsite. 'wonderful' and 'Mass' are read with moderate confidence. The '***' after 'As cancelas' may denote a campsite rating. 'St. Rocca' could alternatively be 'St. Roca' or 'St. Rocco' — written as 'St. Rocca' in the text. 'Portomarin' could also be 'Portomarin' (a well-known flooded village in Galicia, Spain, on the Camino de Santiago pilgrimage route). No year is explicitly stated on this page; based on volume context (1989–2003) and the Camino de Santiago content, year cannot be pinpointed from this page alone.
